title: Pepperpot soup
subtitle: Jamaican dark meat-and-greens broth with okra and allspice
excerpt: A thick, deeply spiced Jamaican soup of slow-cooked beef, wilted greens, and okra in a peppery allspice broth. Dark, hearty, and warming — the Jamaican equivalent of the restorative pot-au-feu.
sourceNotes: Jamaican pepperpot soup is distinct from the Amerindian cassareep-based pepperpot of Guyana and Trinidad. The Jamaican version is a simpler broth of meat and greens seasoned with allspice, scotch bonnet, and thyme. Traditional versions use callaloo, cocoyam, and yam; this recipe uses kale, okra, and sweet potato as widely available substitutes. The colour and depth come from the long cooking of the beef and the allspice, not from cassareep.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the kale, which loses texture. Add fresh kale when reheating. The broth and beef freeze well for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The soup is better the next day once the flavours have melded. Reheat gently with a splash of water if it has thickened too much.

content.text: Pepperpot is not a recipe most people outside the Caribbean know well, which is a shame because it deserves a wider audience. It is the Jamaican version of the slow-cooked pot: a thick, dark, meaty broth that gets its character from allspice, scotch bonnet, and long cooking rather than from cassava-based cassareep (that is the Guyanese version — a different dish entirely). The Jamaican pepperpot is simpler, more herb-forward, and completely satisfying on a cold evening.

The beef needs at least 75 minutes of slow simmering before it is tender; brisket has enough collagen to give the broth body without becoming dry. The greens go in at the end so they retain some colour and texture. The okra dissolves partly into the broth, thickening it naturally.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into chunks — yam or cocoyam if available

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Brown the {{beef-brisket}} of beef brisket in batches, 2–3 minutes per side, until dark on all sides. Set aside. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{allspice}} of allspice to the pot, stir for 30 seconds, then return the beef. Add the spring onion bundle, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, and {{peppercorns-black}} black peppercorns. Pour over enough cold water to cover by 5 cm — about 1.5 litres. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer. Skim any foam from the surface. Cover and cook for 60 minutes.

After 60 minutes, add the {{sweet-potato}} of sweet potato chunks and {{okra}} of sliced okra. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer uncovered for 20 minutes until the sweet potato is tender and the okra has begun to thicken the broth. Remove the spring onion bundle and thyme. Stir in the {{kale}} of torn kale and cook for 5 minutes until wilted. Taste for seasoning and remove the scotch bonnet before serving.

items.cause: Lid kept on throughout; okra not added
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 15–20 minutes to reduce the broth. The okra is the main natural thickener — make sure it is included.

items.cause: Brisket cut too large; heat too low
items.fix: Continue simmering for another 30 minutes. Brisket takes time; large chunks can need 2 hours. The broth should be at a steady, visible simmer, not a bare blip.
Ladle out a cup of broth and discard it, then add cold water to dilute. Add a spoonful of unsalted butter to round out the heat.

items.cause: Stalks left in; added too early
items.fix: Strip the leaves from the stalks and discard the stalks. Add the leaves in the final 5 minutes only — they need very little cooking.

content.text: Pepperpot is Sunday food in the Jamaican home kitchen: a pot that goes on in the morning and sits, occasionally stirred, until mid-afternoon. The deep colour comes from the allspice and the long cooking of the beef rather than from any added colouring. Served with white rice or hard dough bread to soak up the broth, it is the kind of meal that restores something. The Guyanese pepperpot made with cassareep is a related but entirely distinct dish — darker, sweeter, and eaten specifically at Christmas.
